story:
Anita Cable never truly expected to come back. The forms Dr. Costa had her sign said reanimation wasn’t possible yet. And then there was the glioblastoma. But Molly asked, and how could she refuse her little girl? “I’ll wait for you, Mom,” Molly had whispered as the cryo-capsule closed.

Anita wakes to rain against floor-to-ceiling windows, a soft recliner, slippers, a clean orange carpet, and a man in an indigo suit. Dennis—kind-eyed, clipboard ready. She can see. She’s warm. No pain. She touches the scars where surgeons once cut; there’s hair now. Strong legs. A clear head. “Is the cancer gone?” she asks. Dennis’s smile is relieved and sad. “We have to assume it is. I’m not a doctor—I’m a fellow patient.”

They walk to a cafeteria. Floating blue letters display schedules. Anita reaches for them; they’re only light. “We didn’t have these,” she says. “We had TV.” Dennis tilts his head at the old word as if listening to a ghost. When she asks the year, he hesitates. “We don’t know exactly. I went under in 2101. The youngest went in 2248. The systems that tracked absolute dates didn’t survive cleanly, but from surviving logs, equipment decay, and environmental changes, best guess… mid to late 3000s.”

Why doesn’t the news crush her? Sedatives, he warns—the shock will come. Then he gives her the worst of it: while they slept, something happened. War, plague, entropy—no one knows. Humanity is gone. The cryonics tower holds only revived patients, hundreds of them. “If everyone’s dead,” she asks, “who brought us back?” “We’re not alone,” Dennis says. Aliens found the facilities, deciphered the tech, and repaired enough of the automated systems to revive us safely. They maintain the building, the food, the power. Communication is indirect; culturally, they’re difficult to comprehend. To them, Earth was empty. Waking humans is science—preservation, de-extinction.

At the communal welcome in Hall 17, faces blur. That night the truth finally punches through, and Anita screams. Others gather, proof against the worst choices. They have all stood at that same edge.

Weeks pass. She asks to see the aliens. “In time,” Dennis says—bio precautions, protocols. He shows her children racing the halls. “They’re not patients,” Renee explains. “They were born here.” Reproduction is encouraged—ethically, with consent—and still it makes Anita’s skin prickle. But the children don’t carry yesterday’s grief. Outside, the rain is not quite rain; the air has changed. No exit without a sealed suit. “In time,” Dennis repeats, recognizing the look in her eyes. She insists she’s fine. She isn’t.

She starts visiting the ground floor, palms against the glass, then against the door. One day, she tries the emergency release, and red lights begin flashing. An airlock system engages, sealing the inner door while decontamination protocols activate. She backs away quickly, realizing the aliens’ safeguards are more sophisticated than she’d imagined. She picks a Monday—the community’s comfort-word for a calendar that no longer fits. She is kind to everyone that day. She plans to find another way out quietly during some future lapse in routine or maintenance and let the world take her.

At the door, she sends Renee for coffee. Dennis arrives, breathless with luck. He’s found a way to play an old storage drive labeled with her name. People once recorded messages for sleepers. Would she like to watch?

In a dim room, the screen lights a book-lined office. An elderly woman smiles, voice rough. “Hello, Anita. It’s me—Molly.” She never stopped hoping. She worked with elephants for decades. She changed names. She lived. She recorded hours—for herself, for her mother—memories and stories and family. Then: “Do you want to meet your grandkids?”

Anita can’t answer—only weep. The ache at the door loosens. The future didn’t end with her. She may never touch Molly again; Earth is no longer solely human; the aliens remain distant caretakers. But the line goes on. Mondays can still mean something. “All the time in the world, baby,” she whispers, leaning toward the screen, choosing, for now, to live.
